tasks.title: Fix race condition in concurrent POST requests
tasks.description: Concurrent POST requests to /__see-my-clicks can silently lose clicks. Both callbacks can execute readData before either calls writeData, causing the second write to clobber the first.
tasks.details: In src/server.js:129-191, the POST handler reads the request body asynchronously via req.on("end", ...). If two Alt+Clicks fire in rapid succession, both callbacks can read clicked.json before either writes, so the second write overwrites the first. Fix by serializing writes with a simple promise queue, or accumulate pending writes and flush at end of turn.

subtasks.title: Analyze current async POST handler race condition
subtasks.description: Examine the POST handler in src/server.js:129-191 to understand how req.on('end', ...) callbacks can interleave, causing readData/writeData race conditions that lose click data.
subtasks.details: Read server.js:129-191 to map out the async flow: req.on('data', ...) accumulates body chunks, req.on('end', ...) parses JSON and calls readData, then writeData. Document the race: two concurrent requests can both readData before either writeData, causing the second write to clobber the first. Identify all writeData call sites in the POST handler to determine scope of serialization needed.
subtasks.testStrategy: Review code flow and create a diagram or comment block explaining the race condition scenario with timeline of two concurrent requests.

subtasks.title: Design and implement promise queue for write serialization
subtasks.description: Create a simple promise queue mechanism to serialize all writeData calls in the POST handler, ensuring writes happen sequentially without interleaving.
subtasks.details: Implement a lightweight promise queue (15-25 lines) at the top of server.js. Pattern: maintain a promise chain (let writeQueue = Promise.resolve()), and wrap each writeData call in writeQueue = writeQueue.then(() => writeData(...)). This ensures all writes execute in order. Alternative: use a pending writes buffer that accumulates clicks and flushes at end of event loop turn. Choose the simpler approach (promise queue recommended).
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit test the queue mechanism in isolation by queueing multiple writes with delays and verifying execution order.

subtasks.title: Add concurrency test for rapid Alt+Clicks
subtasks.description: Create a Playwright or integration test that fires multiple concurrent POST requests to /__see-my-clicks to verify the race condition is fixed and no clicks are lost.
subtasks.details: Write a test (possibly in tests/ directory alongside existing regression test) that simulates rapid Alt+Clicks by sending 5-10 concurrent POST requests with unique click coordinates. After all requests complete, GET the data and verify all clicks are present (no data loss). Use Promise.all to fire requests in parallel. Test should fail before the fix and pass after.
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration test using fetch or Playwright to send concurrent POST requests, then verify count and uniqueness of stored clicks in clicked.json or via GET response.

subtasks.title: Document serialization approach in code comments
subtasks.description: Add clear inline comments in server.js explaining the promise queue pattern and why write serialization is necessary to prevent race conditions.
subtasks.details: Add a block comment above the writeQueue declaration explaining the race condition (two concurrent POSTs can interleave readData/writeData) and the solution (promise queue ensures sequential writes). Add inline comments at each writeQueue usage point to clarify the serialization. Keep comments concise but sufficient for future maintainers to understand the pattern without re-deriving the problem.
subtasks.testStrategy: Code review to ensure comments are clear, accurate, and sufficient for understanding the concurrency fix.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Break down the race condition fix into: (1) analyze current async POST handler pattern in server.js:129-191, (2) design and implement a promise queue or write buffer mechanism to serialize writeData calls, (3) add concurrency test that fires rapid Alt+Clicks to verify fix, (4) document the serialization approach in code comments

tasks.title: Handle RegExp and function values in Vite plugin watch.ignored
tasks.description: The Vite plugin's config() hook only handles Array and string for server.watch.ignored. RegExp or function values fall into the else branch and are silently replaced, discarding the user's existing ignored pattern.
tasks.details: In src/plugin.js:24-32, the else branch replaces the entire ignored value with ["**/.see-my-clicks/**"]. Fix by handling RegExp and function cases explicitly — wrap them in an array alongside the new entry. Example: if ignored is a RegExp, convert to [ignored, entry]. If it's a function, wrap both in an array or compose them.

subtasks.title: Add type checks for RegExp and function in plugin.js
subtasks.description: Add explicit type checking logic in the plugin.js config() hook else branch (lines 24-32) to detect when server.watch.ignored is a RegExp or function value, replacing the current catch-all else that silently discards user patterns.
subtasks.details: In src/plugin.js:24-32, add instanceof RegExp check and typeof === 'function' check before the current else branch. This will identify the two additional types that need special handling beyond Array and string. The checks should be placed as else-if conditions before the final else to maintain the existing logic flow.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests will verify that RegExp and function inputs are correctly identified and enter the appropriate code path rather than the fallback else branch.

subtasks.title: Implement wrapping logic to preserve user patterns
subtasks.description: Implement the logic to wrap RegExp and function values in arrays alongside the new '**/.see-my-clicks/**' entry, preserving the user's existing ignored patterns instead of replacing them.
subtasks.details: For RegExp: convert to array format [ignored, '**/.see-my-clicks/**']. For function: wrap both the original function and the new entry in an array, or compose the functions if Vite supports it. Update the else branch in src/plugin.js:24-32 to use this wrapping logic. The implementation should mirror the Array case (line 26) which uses spread operator to preserve existing entries.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests will validate that RegExp patterns are preserved in an array with the new entry, and function values are wrapped or composed correctly without losing the original ignore behavior.

subtasks.title: Add unit tests for all watch.ignored input types
subtasks.description: Create comprehensive unit tests covering all four input types (Array, string, RegExp, function) for the server.watch.ignored handling in the Vite plugin config() hook.
subtasks.details: Write test cases that verify: (1) Array inputs preserve all existing entries plus new entry, (2) string inputs are converted to array with both entries, (3) RegExp inputs are wrapped in array preserving the pattern, (4) function inputs are wrapped/composed correctly. Tests should assert the final config.server.watch.ignored value matches expected output for each input type. Consider edge cases like undefined/null inputs.
subtasks.testStrategy: Use a testing framework (e.g., Vitest) to mock the plugin config() hook and assert the output config.server.watch.ignored structure for each input type. Verify both that user patterns are preserved and that '**/.see-my-clicks/**' is added in all cases.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Split into: (1) add type checks for RegExp and function in plugin.js:24-32 else branch, (2) implement wrapping logic to preserve user patterns alongside new entry, (3) add unit tests covering Array/string/RegExp/function cases for watch.ignored handling

tasks.title: Prevent accidental data loss on plain GET to /__see-my-clicks
tasks.description: A plain GET /__see-my-clicks (no query param) writes { sessions: [] } to disk and returns the data, permanently clearing all click data. Any browser, curl, or proxy that fetches the endpoint directly will destroy all stored sessions silently.
tasks.details: In src/server.js:193-207, the consume-and-clear semantic is invisible and undocumented. Fix options: (1) require an explicit ?clear=true param to clear data, (2) remove the consume behavior from GET entirely and use DELETE for clearing, or (3) document it prominently. Option 1 or 2 preferred.

subtasks.title: Decide on API safety approach and document decision
subtasks.description: Choose between requiring explicit ?clear=true param, using DELETE for clearing, or prominent documentation. Recommend option 1 (require ?clear=true) to prevent accidental data loss while maintaining backward compatibility with ?keep param.
subtasks.details: Analyze the current GET handler behavior at server.js:193-207. The current logic clears data unless ?keep is present, which is dangerous. Option 1 (require ?clear=true) is preferred because it makes the destructive action explicit while preserving the ?keep parameter for read-only access. Document the decision and rationale for the implementation in subsequent subtasks.
subtasks.testStrategy: No testing required for decision phase. Document the chosen approach and rationale.

subtasks.title: Modify GET handler to require explicit ?clear=true param
subtasks.description: Update the GET /__see-my-clicks endpoint handler in server.js:193-207 to only clear data when ?clear=true is explicitly present. Default behavior (no query params) should be read-only.
subtasks.details: Change the conditional logic from 'clear unless ?keep is present' to 'clear only if ?clear=true is present'. The new behavior: (1) ?clear=true → consume and clear data, (2) ?keep or no params → read-only, return data without clearing. This requires modifying the query parameter check and ensuring the writeData call only executes when clear=true. Approximately 5-10 lines of code change in the GET handler block.
subtasks.testStrategy: Add tests for three scenarios: (1) GET with no params returns data without clearing, (2) GET with ?clear=true returns data and clears storage, (3) GET with ?keep returns data without clearing (backward compatibility).

subtasks.title: Update client-source.js and verify no breaking changes
subtasks.description: Review client-source.js for any fetch calls to /__see-my-clicks endpoint and update them if they rely on consume-on-GET behavior. Verify no other client code depends on the old default clearing behavior.
subtasks.details: Search client-source.js for fetch calls to the endpoint. Check if any code expects data to be cleared on a plain GET request. If found, update those calls to include ?clear=true. Also verify that the ?keep parameter usage is still functional. Run the test suite to ensure no existing functionality breaks. If no client code relies on consume-on-GET, this subtask mainly involves verification and testing the new behavior end-to-end.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run existing tests and add integration tests that verify: (1) client can read data without clearing it, (2) client can explicitly clear data with ?clear=true, (3) ?keep parameter still works as expected. Test with browser automation to ensure real-world usage patterns work correctly.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Break into: (1) decide approach: require ?clear=true param vs DELETE-only clearing vs prominent docs (recommend option 1), (2) modify GET handler in server.js:193-207 to check for explicit clear param, (3) update client-source.js fetch calls if needed and add tests for new behavior

tasks.title: Add cross-reference comments for duplicate color palette
tasks.description: COLOR_PALETTE in server.js:5-13 and SESSION_COLORS in client-source.js:26-35 are byte-for-byte identical 8-element arrays. Changes to one must be mirrored in the other.
tasks.details: Since client-source.js is a self-contained browser IIFE that cannot import from Node modules, the duplication is structural. Add explicit comments in both files: "// Keep in sync with COLOR_PALETTE in src/server.js" and "// Keep in sync with SESSION_COLORS in src/client-source.js".

subtasks.title: Verify COLOR_PALETTE and SESSION_COLORS are byte-for-byte identical

subtasks.testStrategy: Read server.js to confirm comment is present and correctly positioned above COLOR_PALETTE array

subtasks.title: Add cross-reference comment in client-source.js above SESSION_COLORS

subtasks.details: Add the comment '// Keep in sync with COLOR_PALETTE in src/server.js' on line 25 (just before the SESSION_COLORS declaration at line 26). This alerts developers working on the client code that changes must be mirrored in the server.

subtasks.title: Add architectural justification comment explaining why duplication exists
subtasks.description: Add a brief explanatory comment in client-source.js explaining why SESSION_COLORS cannot import from server.js (IIFE constraint).
subtasks.details: Extend the comment in client-source.js to explain the architectural reason: '// Keep in sync with COLOR_PALETTE in src/server.js\n// Duplicated because this file is a self-contained browser IIFE that cannot import Node modules'. This documents the decision and prevents future refactoring attempts that would break the architecture.

tasks.title: Eliminate O(n*m) loop in updateSessionColor
tasks.description: In client-source.js:516-529, for every marker ID, the code linearly scans all of allClickData to find the matching entry. This nested loop is unnecessary.
tasks.details: Since markers[clickId] already stores the full data object, attach sessionId to the marker when it is created in addMarker (one-line change). Then the inner loop scanning allClickData can be removed entirely, simplifying the code and improving readability.

subtasks.title: Modify addMarker to attach sessionId to marker object
subtasks.description: Update the addMarker function to store sessionId directly on each marker object when it is created, eliminating the need to scan allClickData later.
subtasks.details: In client-source.js, locate the addMarker function where marker objects are created. Add a single line to attach sessionId to the marker object (likely from the click data being passed in). This is a one-line change that stores sessionId alongside other marker properties like position, timestamp, etc. The sessionId should come from the data object that already contains this information when the marker is being created.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that markers object has sessionId property after creation by inspecting markers in browser console. Test that session color updates still work correctly after this change.

subtasks.title: Simplify updateSessionColor to use stored sessionId
subtasks.description: Refactor updateSessionColor function at lines 516-529 to remove the nested loop that scans allClickData, instead using the sessionId now stored directly on marker objects.
subtasks.details: In client-source.js:516-529, replace the current nested loop implementation with direct access to sessionId from markers[clickId]. The outer loop over Object.keys(markers) remains, but the inner loop scanning allClickData to find matching entries can be completely removed. Instead, read sessionId directly from the marker object. This simplifies the logic from O(n*m) to O(n) and improves code readability significantly.
subtasks.testStrategy: Test session color changes by recording clicks across multiple sessions, then changing session colors in the UI. Verify all markers update to correct colors. Check browser console for any errors. Performance can be validated with large datasets if available.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Split into: (1) modify addMarker function to attach sessionId to marker object when creating marker, (2) simplify updateSessionColor function at lines 516-529 to remove inner loop scanning allClickData by using stored sessionId directly from markers object

tasks.title: Cache client script in server.js middleware
tasks.description: getClientScript() in server.js:125 does fs.readFileSync on every GET to /client.js. In plugin.js the script is read once and cached, but middleware-mode users (Express, Webpack, SvelteKit) re-read from disk on every page load.
tasks.details: Cache the result of getClientScript() in a module-level variable inside client.js, or move the caching into createMiddleware. The script content doesn't change at runtime so there's no reason to re-read it.

subtasks.title: Add module-level cache variable in client.js
subtasks.description: Add a module-level variable to cache the client script content after first read, preventing repeated fs.readFileSync calls.
subtasks.details: In src/client.js, add a module-level variable (e.g., `let cachedScript = null;`) before the getClientScript function. This will store the script content after the first read.

subtasks.title: Modify getClientScript to use cached value
subtasks.description: Update getClientScript function to return cached value if available, otherwise read from disk and cache the result.
subtasks.details: In src/client.js:12-14, modify the getClientScript function to check if cachedScript is null. If null, read from disk using fs.readFileSync and store in cachedScript. Return cachedScript. Pattern: if (!cachedScript) { cachedScript = fs.readFileSync(...); } return cachedScript;
subtasks.testStrategy: Manual testing: verify that multiple calls to getClientScript return the same cached value

subtasks.title: Remove redundant caching in plugin.js
subtasks.description: Remove the module-level clientScript variable from plugin.js since caching is now handled in client.js.
subtasks.details: In src/plugin.js:15, remove the line `const clientScript = getClientScript();` and update line 43 to call `getClientScript()` directly in the transformIndexHtml return statement. This eliminates redundant caching now that client.js handles it.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that Vite plugin still injects the script correctly and doesn't re-read from disk on each page load

subtasks.title: Verify middleware no longer re-reads on every request
subtasks.description: Test that server.js middleware now serves cached script without disk reads on subsequent requests.
subtasks.details: In src/server.js:125, verify that getClientScript() now returns the cached value. Test by making multiple GET requests to /__see-my-clicks/client.js and confirming no repeated fs.readFileSync calls occur (can use fs.readFileSync spy or manual verification).
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration test: make multiple HTTP GET requests to /client.js endpoint and verify response is identical and fast (no disk I/O delay)

subtasks.title: Update test to verify caching behavior
subtasks.description: Ensure existing regression test in tests/regression/modal-resize.spec.mjs still works with the new caching mechanism.
subtasks.details: In tests/regression/modal-resize.spec.mjs:4, the test already calls getClientScript() once and stores it in CLIENT_SCRIPT. Verify this still works correctly with the new caching. No changes needed to the test itself, but run it to confirm the cached script content is identical to the previous uncached version.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run npm test:regression to verify modal-resize.spec.mjs passes with cached script

tasks.title: Add cross-reference comments for duplicate ID generation
tasks.description: The same Date.now().toString(36) + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 7) expression appears in both server.js:27 (inside generateId()) and client-source.js:1157 (inlined in captureElement).
tasks.details: Add comments in both locations cross-referencing the other. Consider whether client-generated IDs could clash with server-generated IDs and document the decision.

subtasks.title: Add cross-reference comments in server.js and client-source.js
subtasks.description: Add comments at both ID generation locations (server.js:27 in generateId() and client-source.js:1157 in captureElement) that cross-reference each other, noting that the same Date.now().toString(36) + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 7) expression is used in both places.
subtasks.details: In server.js:27 (inside generateId function), add comment: '// Keep in sync with ID generation in captureElement() in src/client-source.js:1157'. In client-source.js:1157 (inline ID generation in captureElement), add comment: '// Keep in sync with generateId() in src/server.js:27'. This ensures developers are aware of the duplication when modifying either location.
subtasks.testStrategy: Manual verification: read both files and confirm comments are present at the correct line numbers and accurately reference the other location.

subtasks.title: Analyze and document client/server ID collision safety
subtasks.description: Determine whether client-generated IDs (client-source.js:1157) could clash with server-generated IDs (server.js:27), analyze the probability and implications, and document the decision with a comment explaining why the current approach is or is not safe.
subtasks.details: Both use Date.now().toString(36) + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 7). Since Date.now() returns milliseconds since epoch and both client and server execute in similar timeframes, collision is theoretically possible if both generate an ID in the same millisecond with the same random suffix (5 chars from Math.random() provides ~60 million combinations). Calculate collision probability and document findings. Add a comment in one or both locations explaining: 'ID collision between client and server is extremely unlikely due to timestamp precision and 5-char random suffix (~1 in 60M per millisecond). If collision prevention is required, consider adding a client/server prefix.' If collision is deemed a real risk, note whether changes are needed.
subtasks.testStrategy: Review the added documentation comment for technical accuracy. Optionally write a small script to simulate concurrent ID generation and verify collision probability is negligible in practice.

tasks.expansionPrompt: Break into: (1) add cross-reference comments at server.js:27 (generateId) and client-source.js:1157 (inline in captureElement), (2) document whether client/server ID collision is possible and if the current approach is safe

tasks.title: Extract HTTP verb handlers from server.js middleware
tasks.description: seeMyClicksMiddleware in server.js:110-307 handles POST, GET, DELETE, and PUT in one 200-line if/else chain with 4x duplicated error handling (try/catch with writeHead(500) pattern).
tasks.details: Extract handlePost, handleGet, handleDelete, handlePut functions and a shared sendJson(res, status, body) helper. Replace the cascading if/else with a dispatch table. This reduces the function from ~200 lines to a clear dispatcher and eliminates the 4-way duplicated error pattern.

subtasks.title: Extract sendJson helper for response handling
subtasks.description: Create a shared sendJson(res, status, body) helper function to eliminate the 4-way duplicated error handling pattern with writeHead(500) across POST, GET, DELETE, and PUT handlers.
subtasks.details: Extract the common response pattern (res.writeHead + res.end with JSON.stringify) into a reusable sendJson(res, status, body) helper function. This helper should handle setting the correct status code, content-type header, and JSON serialization. Place it before the seeMyClicksMiddleware function in server.js. This eliminates the duplicated try/catch error handling pattern that appears 4 times in the current implementation.
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify that the helper correctly sets headers and serializes JSON by running existing tests. Check that error responses still return 500 status with proper JSON formatting.

subtasks.title: Extract handlePost, handleGet, handleDelete, handlePut functions
subtasks.description: Extract four separate handler functions from the 200-line if/else chain in seeMyClicksMiddleware, one for each HTTP verb (POST, GET, DELETE, PUT).
subtasks.details: Create four separate functions: handlePost(req, res), handleGet(req, res), handleDelete(req, res), and handlePut(req, res). Each function should contain the logic currently in the corresponding if/else branch (lines 129-307). All handlers should use the sendJson helper from subtask 1 for responses and error handling. Preserve the exact behavior of each endpoint including request body parsing, file operations, and response formatting.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run existing endpoint tests for all four HTTP methods to ensure behavior is preserved. Verify that request parsing, file I/O, and response formatting remain unchanged.

subtasks.title: Replace cascading if/else with dispatch table
subtasks.description: Replace the 200-line cascading if/else chain (lines 129-307) with a clean dispatch table or method router that maps HTTP verbs to their handler functions.
subtasks.details: In seeMyClicksMiddleware at lines 129-307, replace the if (req.method === 'POST') / else if (req.method === 'GET') / else if (req.method === 'DELETE') / else if (req.method === 'PUT') chain with a dispatch table approach. Create a handlers object mapping method names to functions: { POST: handlePost, GET: handleGet, DELETE: handleDelete, PUT: handlePut }. Use handlers[req.method]?.(req, res) or similar pattern to dispatch. This reduces the middleware from ~200 lines to a clear, concise dispatcher.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run full test suite to verify all endpoints work correctly with the new dispatch mechanism. Test each HTTP method (POST, GET, DELETE, PUT) and verify unsupported methods still return appropriate errors.

subtasks.title: Verify all endpoints with existing tests
subtasks.description: Run the complete test suite to verify that all HTTP endpoints (POST, GET, DELETE, PUT) still function correctly after the refactoring.
subtasks.details: Execute all existing tests for the see-my-clicks middleware to ensure the refactoring preserved exact behavior. Verify: (1) POST endpoint correctly saves clicks, (2) GET endpoint returns click data, (3) DELETE endpoint removes clicks, (4) PUT endpoint updates sessions/comments. Check that error cases return 500 status with proper JSON, and that unsupported methods are handled correctly. If tests don't exist for all paths, manually test each endpoint or create minimal verification tests.
subtasks.testStrategy: Run npm test or equivalent command to execute the test suite. If coverage gaps exist, perform manual testing with curl or similar tools to verify each endpoint's request/response cycle.

tasks.title: Split PUT handler into separate session and click operations

subtasks.details: Analyze the trade-offs between two approaches: (1) Separate routes provides clearer REST semantics and eliminates branching logic entirely, but requires updating client fetch calls at lines 533 and 821 in client-source.js. (2) Named helper functions (updateSessionColor, updateClickComment) improve readability without breaking the client API but retain the branching dispatch logic. Document the decision and rationale before proceeding with implementation.

tasks.title: Create Overlay UI Theme & Layout Tuner playground
tasks.description: Create a Design Playground that recreates the full see-my-clicks overlay UI as interactive mockups with fake data. No server needed — everything runs client-side.
tasks.details:
Use the /playground skill to create docs/playgrounds/overlay-design.html.

Controls: badge position (bottom-right/left, top-right/left), panel width slider (240-400px), modal width slider (280-440px), session/click count steppers with realistic fake data, marker opacity slider (0.1-1.0), font size scale (0.8x-1.3x).

Presets: "Current defaults", "Compact", "Accessible".

Preview: Mock page with 3-4 dummy elements, positioned markers, open review panel with 2 sessions, comment modal next to one element. All update live as controls change.

Reference source: src/client-source.js lines 51-96 (core elements), 725-802 (modal), 938-964 (session prompt). Extract actual colors (#1e1e2e, #cdd6f4, #8b5cf6, etc.), dimensions, and styles from the source.

Audience: Contributors iterating on UI changes, maintainer evaluating design decisions. Currently the only way to see the UI is to run a full Vite dev server and Alt+Click elements.

tasks.title: Create Capture Data Inspector & Config Simulator playground
tasks.description: Create a Data Explorer playground that lets users explore the clicked.json data format and simulate how config options (maxEntries, expiryMinutes) affect stored data.
tasks.details:
Use the /playground skill to create docs/playgrounds/data-explorer.html.

Three sections:

A) Data Structure Explorer — Pre-populated clicked.json with 3 sessions/8 clicks. Collapsible tree view. Click any field for annotation (from src/index.d.ts interfaces). Presets: "React app with components", "Svelte app with hash classes", "Vue 2 app", "Plain HTML", "Multiple sessions across pages".

B) Config Simulator — Sliders for maxEntries (1-50, default 10) and expiryMinutes (1-1440, default 60). Simulated timeline showing clicks arriving. Expired clicks visually fade out. "Simulate 15 rapid clicks" button. "Advance clock by N minutes" control. Mirrors filterExpiredSessions and slice(-maxEntries) behavior from server.js.

C) AI Instruction Preview — Renders what the AI tool sees for the current data. Action dropdown: suggest-fixes, taskmaster, github-issues, just-report.

Reference source: src/index.d.ts (type definitions), src/server.js (filterExpiredSessions, maxEntries trimming), instructions/claude.md (instruction template), bin/cli.js (action modes).

Audience: Users understanding the data format, contributors touching the data model, users writing custom AI instructions.

tasks.title: Create Architecture & Data Flow code map playground
tasks.description: Create a Code Map playground visualizing the see-my-clicks architecture: 6 source files, their exports, and how data flows from browser click to JSON file to AI tool.
tasks.details:
Use the /playground skill to create docs/playgrounds/architecture.html.

Nodes: client-source.js (browser, with sub-nodes for event listeners, element capture, framework detection, UI components, server communication), client.js (file reader/cache), plugin.js (Vite hooks: config, configureServer, transformIndexHtml), server.js (middleware handlers: handlePost, handleGet, handleDelete, handlePut, updateSessionColor, updateClickComment), index.js (entry re-exports), bin/cli.js (CLI tool detection, instruction templating), clicked.json (data file), AI tool instructions (5 templates).

Edges/data flow: Browser Alt+Click -> client-source.js captureElement() -> fetch POST -> server.js handlePost() -> clicked.json. clicked.json -> AI reads file -> instruction template -> code suggestions. plugin.js -> server.js (creates middleware) + client.js (gets script for injection). cli.js -> instructions/*.md (templates).

Interactive: Click node for exports/line count/role. Click edge for data shape. Toggle "Vite mode" vs "Middleware mode". Highlight paths: "Click capture flow", "Data read flow", "CLI init flow".

Audience: Contributors onboarding to the codebase, potential adopters evaluating internals.

tasks.title: Create Framework Detection Demo playground
tasks.description: Create a Design Playground demonstrating how see-my-clicks detects React, Vue 2/3, and Svelte components from DOM elements — the key differentiating feature.
tasks.details:
Use the /playground skill to create docs/playgrounds/framework-detection.html.

Layout: Split view — left side has mock DOM tree with simulated framework internals, right side shows what see-my-clicks captures.

Framework tabs: React, Vue 3, Vue 2, Svelte, Plain HTML. Each with 3-4 nested mock components.

Click any element to see: which detection function fires (getSvelteComponent, getReactComponent, getVueComponent from client-source.js), the fiber/instance walk pattern, the resulting { framework, name, file } object, how findElement relocates elements using fallback strategies (exact selector -> stripped hash selector -> data-* attrs -> ID -> component+tag+text).

Edge cases panel: Svelte hash class stripping (stripHashClasses), React without displayName, Vue 2 vs Vue 3 differences, elements with no framework detected.

Presets: "React Next.js app", "SvelteKit app", "Vue 3 Nuxt app", "Mixed micro-frontends".

Reference source: src/client-source.js lines 1014-1086 (detection functions), 565-633 (findElement fallbacks).

Audience: Users evaluating framework support, contributors adding new framework detection (Angular, Solid, etc.), users debugging "Unknown" component names.

tasks.title: Fix wrong GitHub URL in playground index footer
tasks.description: The index.html playground footer links to https://github.com/anthropics/see-my-clicks which is wrong. Should link to the correct repository URL.
tasks.details: In docs/playgrounds/index.html line 229, the footer contains: `see-my-clicks`. The "anthropics" org is incorrect. Update to the correct GitHub repository URL.

tasks.title: Fix "browser extension" wording in data-explorer playground
tasks.description: The data-explorer.html findings panel context blurb incorrectly calls see-my-clicks a "browser extension". It's a Vite plugin / Express middleware, not a browser extension.
tasks.details: In docs/playgrounds/data-explorer.html line 409, the text reads: "The clicked.json format is the contract between the browser extension and AI tools." Change "browser extension" to "capture tool" or "Vite plugin" to accurately describe see-my-clicks.

tasks.title: Fix Add button disabled state inconsistency across playgrounds
tasks.description: The "+ Add" button in the findings panel has inconsistent disabled/enabled logic across the 4 playgrounds. Some disable based on note content, some on selected state, and some never disable at all.
tasks.details:
Issues found:

1. **architecture.html**: The `add-finding-btn` is NEVER disabled. The `addGeneralFinding()` function silently returns on empty input, but the button stays visually enabled. Should disable when textarea is empty, matching overlay-design.html pattern.

2. **data-explorer.html**: The `add-finding-btn` is disabled based on `totalClicks === 0` (line 1128-1130), not textarea content. This means with a preset loaded, the button stays enabled even with an empty textarea. `addFinding()` silently returns on empty note (line 1048). Should also check textarea content.

3. **overlay-design.html**: Correctly disables based on textarea content via input event listener (line 1138-1142, 1152). This is the reference pattern.

4. **framework-detection.html**: Disables based on `!state.selectedNode` (line 1718-1719). Note is optional here (allowed to be null). This is intentionally different but could confuse — consider requiring at least a note OR a selected node.

Fix: Add input event listeners to architecture.html and data-explorer.html finding textareas that disable the button when empty, matching the overlay-design.html pattern.

tasks.title: Fix Mixed micro-frontends preset tab highlighting in framework-detection playground
tasks.description: The "Mixed micro-frontends" preset sets `state.activeFramework = 'mixed'` but 'mixed' is not in the FRAMEWORKS array, so no framework tab highlights when this preset is active.
tasks.details:
In docs/playgrounds/framework-detection.html:

1. Line 1451-1454: The mixed preset sets `state.activeFramework = 'mixed'`
2. Line 755: `FRAMEWORKS = ['react', 'vue3', 'vue2', 'svelte', 'html']` — no 'mixed' entry
3. Line 1420-1421: Tab rendering checks `state.activeFramework === fw` — no match for 'mixed'

Result: When "Mixed micro-frontends" is selected, no framework tab is highlighted, creating a confusing UI state.

Fix options:
- Add a 'mixed' entry to FRAMEWORKS with a tab
- Or deselect all framework tabs explicitly when mixed is active (add visual indication like "mixed" badge in the tab bar)
- Or render the mixed tree without changing the framework tabs (keep the last selected tab active)

tasks.title: Unify CSS variable strategy across playgrounds
tasks.description: The 4 playgrounds use inconsistent CSS strategies — some use :root custom properties, some hardcode hex values throughout. This makes palette changes error-prone.
tasks.details:
Current state:
- **overlay-design.html**: Uses `:root` CSS variables (`--smc-purple`, `--smc-bg-dark`, etc.) for some styles, but hardcodes in JS-rendered elements
- **data-explorer.html**: Hardcodes all hex colors everywhere (no CSS variables)
- **architecture.html**: Hardcodes all hex colors (no CSS variables)
- **framework-detection.html**: Uses `:root` CSS variables (`--bg`, `--text`, `--accent`, etc.) consistently in CSS

Recommended: Adopt framework-detection.html's approach — define a shared `:root` variable set at the top of each playground. The variable names should be consistent across all playgrounds (e.g., `--bg`, `--text`, `--accent`, `--surface`, `--border`, framework-specific colors). This makes it easy to tweak the dark theme palette.

Low priority since these are standalone HTML files, but improves maintainability.

tasks.title: Complete index.d.ts type definitions
tasks.description: index.d.ts only declares 4 of 13 fields in CapturedElementData. Missing fields: clickId, timestamp, elementId, classList, boundingBox, component, parentChain, url, viewportSize. Also missing CaptureSession shape (id, name, color, startedAt, clicks[]) and CaptureStore shape ({ sessions: CaptureSession[] }).
tasks.details:
Discovered by comparing data-explorer playground against real code. The playground documents the full data shape accurately but index.d.ts is severely behind.

Current state (src/index.d.ts): Only declares selector, tagName, textContent, attributes in CapturedElementData.

Missing fields to add:
- clickId: string
- timestamp: string (ISO 8601)
- elementId: string
- classList: string[]
- boundingBox: { x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number }
- component: { name: string, file: string, framework: string } | null
- parentChain: Array<{ tagName: string, id: string, classList: string[], component: { name: string, file: string, framework: string } | null }>
- url: string
- viewportSize: { width: number, height: number }

Also add/fix:
- CaptureSession: { id: string, name: string, color: string, startedAt: string, clicks: CapturedElementData[] }
- CaptureStore: { sessions: CaptureSession[] }

Reference: src/client-source.js:1149-1195 (captureElement), src/server.js:143-151 (session creation)
tasks.testStrategy: Run tsc --noEmit on a test file that imports and uses all exported types. Verify all fields from a real clicked.json match the declared types.

tasks.title: Update architecture playground after refactoring
tasks.description: The architecture playground is significantly outdated after recent refactoring. Line counts, CLI role, POST body shape, session schema fields, and edges are all wrong.
tasks.details:
Discrepancies found by comparing architecture.html against actual source:

1. **Line counts wrong for all 6 files**:
   - client-source.js: playground says 1480, actual 1481
   - client.js: says 25, actual 21
   - plugin.js: says 90, actual 52
   - server.js: says 280, actual 315
   - index.js: says 10, actual 3
   - bin/cli.js: says 60, actual 329 (!!!)
   Location: docs/playgrounds/architecture.html:547-589

2. **CLI node role severely outdated**: Playground says "copies AI instruction templates and initializes .see-my-clicks/ directory". Real CLI now supports --action presets (suggest-fixes, taskmaster, github-issues, just-report), multiple tool targets, copy vs append modes, and SvelteKit SSR auto-patching. Line 590-591.

3. **POST body shape wrong** (edge e1, line 613): Shows flat `{ selector, tagName, textContent, boundingRect, attributes, computedStyles, framework, componentName }`. Real shape is `{ data: { clickId, timestamp, tagName, elementId, classList, selector, textContent, boundingBox, component, parentChain, attributes, url, viewportSize }, newSession, sessionName }`. computedStyles doesn't exist, framework/componentName are inside component object.

4. **Session schema wrong**: Field is `startedAt` not `createdAt`, missing `name` field. Line 584.

5. **Edge e12 wrong**: Says CLI creates .see-my-clicks/ dir — actually server.js does this via ensureOutputFile(). 

6. **Missing edge**: server.js imports getClientScript from client.js and serves it at GET /__see-my-clicks/client.js (middleware mode). Not shown.

7. **server.js exports**: Lists filterExpiredSessions() as export but it's internal-only. Only createMiddleware is exported.
tasks.testStrategy: Open architecture.html in browser, verify line counts match wc -l output, verify POST body shape matches captureElement() return, verify all edges reflect actual import/serve relationships.

tasks.title: Update framework-detection playground: add parent-walking, displayName, verifyElement
tasks.description: The framework-detection playground is missing 3 key behaviors from the real code: getFrameworkComponentFromChain() parent-walking, React displayName check, and verifyElement() rejection simulation.
tasks.details:
Discrepancies found by comparing framework-detection.html against src/client-source.js:

1. **getFrameworkComponentFromChain() entirely absent** (HIGH): Real code at client-source.js:1131-1139 walks UP parent elements calling getFrameworkComponent() at each level. Used during click capture (line 1190) and findElement step 5 (line 621). The playground only simulates single-element detection. This is a core behavior users need to understand.

2. **React displayName check missing** (MEDIUM): Real code checks `node.type.displayName || node.type.name` (client-source.js:1087). Playground only checks `name`. Means playground can't demo detection of React.memo/React.forwardRef wrapped components.

3. **verifyElement() not functionally simulated** (MEDIUM): Real code calls verifyElement(el, data) at EVERY findElement step (client-source.js:555-563, 574, 583, 600, 609, 625). It compares truncateText(el.innerText, 100) with stored textContent. Playground marks it cosmetically but doesn't model text-match rejection causing fallback.

4. **findElement step 3 only tries first data-* attr** (LOW): Playground line 1338 only checks dataAttrs[0]. Real code iterates ALL data-* attributes (client-source.js:593-601).

5. **_debugSource.fileName not modeled** (LOW): Real React detection reads node._debugSource.fileName (only in dev mode). Playground uses flat file property on mock data.
tasks.testStrategy: Open playground, click elements with no direct framework markers — parent-walking should find component on ancestor. Click a React.memo wrapped component — displayName should be found. Test findElement with multiple data-* attrs — all should be tried.

tasks.title: Update overlay playground: add missing interactive states and behaviors
tasks.description: The overlay playground is missing several real UI behaviors: off-route dimming, hidden session opacity, marker-row hover linking, edit-on-click, empty state, and ARIA attributes.
tasks.details:
Discrepancies found by comparing overlay-design.html against src/client-source.js:

**Medium severity:**
- Panel missing off-route click entry dimming (real uses color:#6c7086 for text, #45475a for numbers — client-source.js:397-400)
- Panel missing hidden session opacity:0.4 (client-source.js:343)
- Marker z-index stacking wrong: playground markers have no z-index container; real markers inherit 999997 from __smc-markers parent (client-source.js:101)
- Modal max-height uses 100% instead of 100vh (overlay-design.html:856 vs client-source.js:735)

**Low severity:**
- No "No captures yet" empty state (client-source.js:223-224)
- No hidden eye icon variant / slashed eye SVG (client-source.js:344-350)
- Row hover only toggles row background; real code also highlights corresponding marker with scale 1.5 + glow (client-source.js:274-278)
- Click rows not clickable for editing — real code opens edit modal (client-source.js:261-268)
- Modal always shows Delete button; real code hides it for new clicks, only shows in edit mode (client-source.js:749, 917)
- Component access pattern: playground uses c.component (string), real uses c.component.name (object) (client-source.js:385-387)
- Missing ARIA attributes on modal and session prompt (role="dialog", aria-modal, aria-label — client-source.js:728-730, 939-941)
- Missing modal focus trap / Tab cycling (client-source.js:783-799)

**Info (by design for playground tuning):**
- fontScale slider is playground-only (real code has no font scaling)
- Badge position configurable in playground but hardcoded to bottom-right in real code
tasks.testStrategy: Open playground, verify off-route entries are dimmed, hidden sessions show at 0.4 opacity with slashed eye, row hover highlights marker, clicking row opens modal, empty state shown when no captures.

tasks.title: Fix data-explorer playground: maxEntries trimming and phantom action modes
tasks.description: The data-explorer playground shows wrong maxEntries trimming behavior and lists AI action modes that don't exist in the actual instruction templates.
tasks.details:
Discrepancies found by comparing data-explorer.html against src/server.js and instructions/:

1. **maxEntries trimming scope wrong** (HIGH): Playground shows maxEntries trimming ALL sessions at read-time (per-session limit applied when data is read). Real server.js only trims the ACTIVE session at write-time (src/server.js:153-157) — when a new click is added, only that session's clicks are trimmed to maxEntries. Historical sessions keep all their clicks. The algorithm box in the playground conflates filterExpiredSessions() and maxEntries trimming into one function.

2. **Phantom action modes** (HIGH): Playground shows 4 AI action modes: suggest-fixes, taskmaster, github-issues, just-report. The actual instruction templates in instructions/ only implement suggest-fixes. The other 3 (taskmaster, github-issues, just-report) exist as CLI --action presets but don't have corresponding instruction template content.

3. **parentChain missing component field** (MEDIUM): Playground preset data omits the component field in parentChain entries. Real code at client-source.js:1149-1154 always includes component (either the detection result or null).

4. **Algorithm display**: The "How It Works" box conflates filterExpiredSessions and maxEntries into a single function. In reality, filterExpiredSessions (server.js) handles expiry and maxEntries trimming is a separate step in handlePost.
tasks.testStrategy: Open data-explorer, load a preset, verify the algorithm explanation matches actual server.js behavior. Check that only suggest-fixes mode is shown (or clearly mark others as planned/future).
